coredata.dc:title: High-performance, recyclable ultrafiltration membranes from P4VP-assisted dispersion of flame-resistive boron nitride nanotubes
coredata.dc:creator: Lim, Hongjin
Suh, Bong Lim
Kim, Myung Jong
Yun, Hongseok
Kim, Jihan
Kim, Bumjoon J.
Jang, Se Gyu
coredata.dc:description: Regenerable ultrafiltration membranes were fabricated via filtration of thermally-stable, highly dispersed boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs). The highly debundled BNNTs were produced by employing judiciously-chosen poly(4-vinylpyridine) (P4VP) as an efficient steric stabilizer. Density functional theory calculations showed strong adsorption energies of P4VP monomers on top of the BNNTs, illustrating the role of P4VP stabilizers. High performance of the BNNT ultrafiltration membranes with large permeation flux was demonstrated by exclusion of polystyrene and gold nanoparticles (~ 25nm) with higher than 99% removal efficiency. The BNNT membranes were successfully regenerated and recycled continuously at 450°C due to their excellent mechanical and thermal properties, demonstrating a stark contrast to the membranes from the carbon nanotubes. Our work indicates that unlike the membranes from carbon nanotubes, BNNT membrane can be a promising candidate for the application in cost efficient industrial ultrafiltration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Membrane
Colloidal dispersion
Boron nitride nanotube
Non-covalent functionalization
Stabilizer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Purification of BNNTs and preparation of BNNT dispersions
Characterization of size exclusion efficiency of membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Optical image of BNNTs dispersed in various alcohols as labeled on the top of the vials (C BNNT = 0.2mg/mL). The P4VP to BNNT mass ratio (MP4VP / MBNNT) is 0.1. (b) UV–vis spectra of BNNT dispersion stabilized with various MP4VP / MBNNT values ranging from 0 to 3.0 (C BNNT = 0.4mg/mL). (c) Variation of absorbance values (at 500nm wavelength) of UV–vis spectra in b normalized by the saturation value (MP4VP / MBNNT = 3). (d) and (e) Turbiscan data of BNNT dispersion in MeOH without P4VP (d) and with P4VP (e) (C BNNT = 0.792mg/mL). Almost no change in transmittance (about 1.5% for 24h) is observed through the whole sample height. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Top (left side) and side (right side) views of the optimized structures of P4VP on top of the bridge, hollow, nitrogen and boron BNNT. B, C, N and H are shown in pink, gray, blue and white colors, respectively. (b) Same as (a) but with P2VP on the BNNT. (c) and (d) The binding energy of P4VP and P2VP on the BNNT, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Characterization of BNNT membranes fabricated on glass-fiber supporting filter. a–b) Top-view SEM images of the membranes prepared by (a) P4VP-assisted BNNT dispersion and (b) BNNT dispersion without P4VP agents. Insets are the optical images of the membranes. (c) Thickness variation of the BNNT membranes according to the areal mass of BNNT used for the fabrication. (d) Water permeability variation of the BNNT membranes according to the areal mass of BNNT.

(a) Optical image showing the relative fluorescent intensities of solutions filtered by membranes with various areal masses. MPDx denotes the membrane prepared with P4VP-assisted dispersion with the areal mass of x. (b) Fluorescent spectra of filtered solutions after exclusion of PS latex with different membranes. (excitation wavelength: 455nm) (c) A graph shows the exclusion performance of BNNT membranes. The excluded percentage of PS latex was calculated from the relative fluorescent intensity at 515.9nm compared to the one of initial PS latex dispersion. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a) A graph showing the superior durability of MPDs even under the consecutive cycles of thermal calcination to remove clogged PS latex and reuse for filtration. (b) Optical and SEM images of BNNT MPD after calcination at 450°C for 6h. Optical and SEM images of multi-walled CNT membrane: (c) as-prepared and (d) after calcination. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Schematic illustration of the fabrication process for thermally regenerable ultrafiltration membranes using highly debundled BNNT dispersion with the aid of polymeric dispersing agent (P4VP). Top: BNNT is dispersed in alcohols in the presence of P4VP stabilizers. Bottom: thin BNNT membrane is fabricated by simple filtration of BNNT dispersion on glass-fiber prefilter. The BNNT membrane can successfully exclude the fluorescent-labeled PS latex (25nm in diameter) and it can be regenerated continuously by calcination for reuse.

head.ce:title: High-performance, recyclable ultrafiltration membranes from P4VP-assisted dispersion of flame-resistive boron nitride nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Quantum Composite Research Center, Institute of Advanced Composites Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, Wanju, Jeonbuk 55324, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Applied Quantum Composite Research Center, Institute of Advanced Composites Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Daejeon 34141, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Regenerable ultrafiltration membranes were fabricated via filtration of thermally-stable, highly dispersed boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs). The highly debundled BNNTs were produced by employing judiciously-chosen poly(4-vinylpyridine) (P4VP) as an efficient steric stabilizer. Density functional theory calculations showed strong adsorption energies of P4VP monomers on top of the BNNTs, illustrating the role of P4VP stabilizers. High performance of the BNNT ultrafiltration membranes with large permeation flux was demonstrated by exclusion of polystyrene and gold nanoparticles (~ 25nm) with higher than 99% removal efficiency. The BNNT membranes were successfully regenerated and recycled continuously at 450°C due to their excellent mechanical and thermal properties, demonstrating a stark contrast to the membranes from the carbon nanotubes. Our work indicates that unlike the membranes from carbon nanotubes, BNNT membrane can be a promising candidate for the application in cost efficient industrial ultrafiltration.

Recyclable ultrafiltration membranes having high water flux and excellent size exclusion performance were fabricated from P4VP-assisted dispersion of boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs). The concept of thermally durable and reusable BNNT membrane suggests great potential to resolve important problems such as clogging (or fouling) and corresponding increase in energy consumption, which are common in water purification and ultrafiltration applications.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrafiltration using membranes with pore diameters of 10–100nm has been an emerging technology in desalination of drinking water and food processing, owing to its high selectivity and low operation cost [1–5]. However, the ultrafiltration membranes are vulnerable to membrane fouling by excluded particulates and micro-organisms, resulting in loss of permeation flux and corresponding increase in energy demand [6–8]. Therefore, cleaning and recycling these membranes [9–14] are critical issues that need to be addressed prior to their practical usage including water purification. Often, calcination at high temperature (i.e. 400–500°C) can be an effective way to regenerate the membranes by removing all of the filtered organic impurities and, as such, the thermal stability of the membranes is a critical requirement for their successful long-term usage [15].

Most ultrafiltration membranes are fabricated with polymers such as polyacrylonitrile, polysulfone, cellulose acetate, and regenerated cellulose due to simple processing and low cost in achieving nanometer-sized pores [16]. However, the polymeric membranes are mechanically, chemically, and thermally unstable during membrane regeneration involving either acid/base cleaning or calcination process to remove any of filtered organic matters. Inorganic membranes mainly composed of metal oxides (including anodic aluminum oxide (AAO)) can be used alternatively [17–19], but there are other detrimental issues such as high production cost and dissolution/degradation of metal oxide membranes during operation [20]. For all these reasons, the development of a simple protocol to fabricate ultrafiltration membranes with high mechanical strength, chemical stability, and thermal durability against calcination remains a key challenge.

Recently, boron nitride nanotube (BNNT) has attracted a growing amount of attention as an inorganic analogue of carbon nanotube (CNT) [21] with its excellent properties such as high mechanical strength (elastic modulus ~ 1.18TPa) and chemical inertness against strong acid/base. More importantly, unlike the CNTs, the BNNTs have high temperature tolerance up to 900°C in air, [22,23] which makes them perfectly suitable for the fabrication of reusable membranes by calcination. Despite these advantages, the use of BNNTs has been limited because they are typically synthesized into the form of aggregates bundled by van der Waals attractions among the sidewalls, which hinder their solubilization in most solvents [24]. Therefore, for potential applications including membranes, the BNNTs have to be debundled and dispersed individually in solvents [25–27]. To improve the dispersion stability of BNNTs in solvents, significant efforts have been exerted on the functionalization of BNNTs by covalent attachment using chemical reactions such as harsh acid oxidation, physico-chemical bombardment of ammonia plasma, and hydroxylation with peroxides [28–35]. However, such processes under harsh conditions often damage the outside wall and degrade the intrinsic properties of BNNTs. By contrast, non-covalent functionalization approaches using physical adsorption or wrapping of stabilizing species on BNNTs provide simple and environmentally friendly protocols for stable dispersion of BNNTs without damaging the BNNTs [27,36–39]. Nonetheless, previously reported approaches use expensive conjugated (or bio-) polymers and have limited number of available solvents for dispersing the BNNTs [40–44].

Here, we demonstrate a powerful one-step method to prepare highly robust and efficient ultrafiltration membranes using a sequential process of filtration and re-assembly of highly debundled BNNTs. The membranes are mechanically strong and highly robust against chemical/thermal oxidation and, thus, readily regenerable and recyclable by calcination. A stable dispersion of BNNTs in an organic solvent is prepared in the presence of polymeric stabilizer poly(4-vinylpyridine) (P4VP) ( Scheme 1, figures on the top). The favorable adsorption of P4VP chains on BNNTs was experimentally investigated and also supported by density functional theory (DFT). The excellent long-term stability of P4VP-assisted BNNT dispersion was demonstrated by UV–vis spectroscopy and Turbiscan analysis. The thermally regenerable ultrafiltration membranes were fabricated using simple filtration of BNNT dispersion on glass-fiber prefilter (Scheme 1, figures on the bottom). Interestingly, the thin BNNT membrane with only about 2.6mg BNNT per centimeter square was enough to exclude over 99% of polymer particles and inorganic Au nanoparticles with the size of about 25nm in diameter. Most importantly, we demonstrate that the used BNNT membrane could be regenerated and recycled continuously using a simple calcination process, attributed to the excellent thermal inertness and mechanical properties of the BNNTs. In contrast to the BNNT membranes, the control membranes consisting of CNTs could not be recycled due to their thermal instability.

Steric stabilization by polymer ligands with favorable adsorption on particles provides a simple pathway to disperse the particles without involving any chemical reactions that often damage the particles [45–55]. We chose P4VP as dispersing agents for BNNTs in solvents (i.e., alcohols) because the pyridine group in P4VP is a well-known Lewis base and, thus, P4VPs strongly interact with outer surface of BNNTs. Fig. 1 shows the superior efficiency of P4VP for preparation of stable BNNT dispersion. The pristine BNNTs had very poor solubility in alcohols and they became sedimented on the bottom. The poor solubility of the BNNTs in alcohols is in good agreement with the analysis of thermodynamic parameters listed in Table S1 [26]. By contrast, when P4VP (10wt% relative to the mass of BNNT (0.2mg/mL)) was added as a stabilizer, a remarkably improved dispersion of BNNTs was observed regardless of the alcohol type. In order to determine the optimum amount of the P4VP stabilizers that can efficiently debundle the BNNTs, a series of BNNT dispersions with different P4VP to BNNT mass ratio (MP4VP / MBNNT) from 0.1 to 3.0 in MeOH (C BNNT = 0.4mg/mL) were prepared. As shown in the UV–vis spectra of Fig. 1(b), as the amount of the added P4VP was increased, the absorbance of the BNNT dispersion, which directly relates to the dispersed concentration of the BNNTs, was also increased. The absorbance became saturated when the MP4VP / MBNNT is higher than 2, which indicates that most of the BNNTs are debundled and well dispersed in the solvent. Next, in order to clearly visualize the effect of P4VP for stabilization of the BNNTs, the absorbance values of the samples at 500nm wavelength were plotted and normalized to the saturated one (MP4VP / MBNNT = 3) (Fig. 1(c)). 10% and 50% (w/w) of P4VP compared to the mass of BNNT dispersed 74% and 95% of BNNTs, respectively. Furthermore, when the P4VP to BNNT ratio was higher than the optimum value (MP4VP / MBNNT = 0.5), the absorbance increased in proportion to the concentration of BNNT (see Supporting information Fig. S1a). Therefore, we can conclude that the ratio of 0.5 is the optimum (or minimum) value to achieve stable BNNT dispersion in alcohol.

The long-term stability of colloidal dispersion is one of the most important factors in evaluating the efficiency of a dispersing agent. Fig. 1(d) and (e) show the spectra obtained from Turbiscan, which traces the change of transmittance through the whole height of 20mL vial at a fixed time-interval [56]. Therefore, long-term floatation and sedimentation behavior of solutes in dispersion can be detected by tracing the change of transmission. The unstable purified BNNTs in methanol (MeOH) without P4VP (C BNNT = 0.792mg/mL) were aggregated rapidly and precipitated on the bottom of the vial as shown in the inset of Fig. 1(d). As a result, the transmission intensity near the bottom of the vial (bottom left corner of x-axis) reduced significantly and almost no change in transmittance was observed throughout the characterization period (sample height range from 0 to about 20mm). At the height above 20mm, the transmittance value increased by over 20% after one day due to slow sedimentation of the BNNTs. In contrast to the pristine BNNTs, the BNNTs with P4VP in MeOH (MP4VP / MBNNT = 1, C BNNT = 0.792mg/mL) were homogeneously dispersed and showed almost no change in transmittance (less than 1.5%) throughout the whole sample height and characterization period (Fig. 1(e)). Further long-term stability test was conducted for 7 days and the BNNTs with P4VP showed reduced transmission of less than 4% (Fig. S1b), indicating the excellent dispersion stability of the BNNTs. These observations clearly show the excellent performance of P4VP as an efficient dispersing agent for BNNTs.

The experimental results presented above reveal that P4VP chains interact favorably with the outer surface of BNNTs, leading to reversible binding (or wrapping) of P4VP chains to the BNNTs. Adsorption of P4VP chains is supposed to provide a reasonable screening of van der Waals attraction among nanotubes via steric hindrance. Recently, it was reported that pyridine groups strongly interact with the surface of BNNTs [57]. However, despite its structural similarity, poly(2-vinylpyridine) (P2VP) showed no positive effect on stabilization of BNNT in our control experiment (Fig. S2). To gain deeper insight regarding the favorable interaction of P4VP and BNNT, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed and the results are summarized in Fig. 2 (see Supporting information for the details on the calculation methods).

The DFT optimized structures for the adsorption of P4VP and P2VP monomers on BNNT surface are illustrated in Fig. 2(a) and (b), respectively. In order to find the global minimum energy values, four different sites (i.e. on the bridge, on the hollow, on the nitrogen, on the boron) were used as initial input monomer configurations. For the P4VP monomer, the computed adsorption energies (ΔEads) were −71.79kJ/mol, −60.57kJ/mol, 0.01kJ/mol, and −76.09kJ/mol on the bridge, hollow, nitrogen, and boron, respectively, illustrating that P4VP has a stronger preference to the boron site (Fig. 2(c)). The binding energy values here agree well with the DFT binding energy of pyridine on BNNT (~ −71.13kJ/mol) from a previous study [57]. The good agreement is not surprising given that the nitrogen atom of the heterocyclic ring is negatively charged compared to the carbon and the hydrogen atoms, leading to strong electrostatic interactions with the positively charged boron site of BNNT. In contrast to the P4VP, the P2VP monomer has significantly weaker binding energies with the adsorption energies of −9.31kJ/mol, 3.07kJ/mol, −12.01kJ/mol, and −3.27kJ/mol on the bridge, hollow, nitrogen, and boron site, respectively (Fig. 2(d)). Given the location of the nitrogen atom in the heterocyclic ring for the P2VP monomer, the initial test configuration of the nitrogen atom located above the boron site leads to repulsive interaction, and as such, one can see from Fig. 2(b) that all of the optimized configurations have nitrogen atoms further away from the BNNT surface. These calculation results clearly explain the favorable binding and wrapping of P4VP as well as failure in stabilization of BNNTs with P2VP (Fig. S2).

BNNTs are distinguished by unique properties such as high mechanical strength and excellent chemical resistance. In particular, thermal resistance of BNNTs at over 900°C with no loss of mechanical strength is the most promising property of BNNTs, in contrast to CNTs [58]. In order to emphasize these excellent properties, thermally regenerable membranes with ultra-high filtration performance were prepared by simple vacuum filtration of BNNT dispersion through glass-fiber prefilter. Fig. 3(a) and (b) show the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the top surface of the membranes fabricated from the ethanol dispersions with and without P4VP, respectively (MP4VP / MBNNT = 0.5, areal mass of 3.1mg BNNT per centimeter square). When the membrane was prepared from the P4VP-assisted dispersions of the BNNTs, dense packing of the BNNTs with negligible void space was obtained (Fig. 3(a)), which shows a sharp contrast to the control film fabricated by the dispersion without any P4VP stabilizer (Fig. 3(b)). The dense packing of the BNNT membrane is mainly attributed to the high degree of debundling as evidenced by significant difference in the average diameters of the BNNTs dispersed with (14.6 ± 5.0nm) and without P4VP (33.5 ± 14.1nm) (Fig. S3, measured by analysis of SEM images). The degree of debundling of BNNTs and packing density of the BNNT membrane in the present experimental condition is high enough to fabricate ultrafiltration membrane without any crack formation, which is also different from the bucky paper prepared with the same amount of BNNTs. (optical inset images in Fig. 3(a) and (b))

In order to evaluate the filtration performance of the BNNT membrane (MPD), a series of membranes with different thicknesses were prepared by varying the amounts of BNNTs (MP4VP / MBNNT = 0.5). To generate high flux of the membrane and avoid any contamination, the P4VP agents were removed by calcination at 450°C for 6h. The thickness of the membrane is increased proportionally to the areal mass of BNNTs and as a result, membrane with the BNNT density of approximately 0.65g/cm3 was achieved with the areal mass of 3.1mg/cm2 as shown in Fig. 3(c). The cross-sectional view of the densely-packed BNNTs on the glass fiber supporting filter was also observed by the SEM measurement (Fig. S4(a)). In order to characterize the permeation performance of the prepared MPDs, the hydraulic permeability (or flux) values at the transmembrane pressure of 0.8bar were measured for each sample (Fig. 3(d)). With the increase in the areal mass of the BNNTs, the water permeability values decreased and saturated to the value of about 230L/m2·h at the areal mass of 3.1mg/cm2 (MPD3.1, the number denotes the areal mass of BNNT). We note that despite the hydrophobic surface property of the BNNTs, the water flux (230L/m2 h) of the MPD is much higher compared to the values of the commercialized AAO membranes [20] (59 and 10L/m2 h for average pore sizes of 90 and 40nm, respectively, at transmembrane pressure of 0.8bar), indicating higher porosity of the BNNT membrane.

The size exclusion performance is one of the most important properties of ultrafiltration membrane. To demonstrate the superior performance of the BNNT-based MPDs, model compounds, fluorescent-labeled polystyrene latex (about 24.5 ± 7.0nm, measured by SEM in Fig. S5) dispersed in water was filtered with MPD2.6 at the transmembrane pressure of 0.8bar using the filtration kit (Fig. S6(a)). The excluded PS latex cake was clearly shown on the top of MPD under illumination of 365nm UV-light and the filtration efficiency was measured to be as high as 99.9% (Fig. S6(b)). Next, we investigated the performance of membrane by varying the thickness of the membrane to determine the lowest exclusion size limit. Fig. 4(a) displays the optical image of the aqueous solution after filtration by labeled MPDs illuminated by UV-light from the bottom of UV–vis cuvettes. The image clearly shows the dependence of exclusion performance on the membrane thickness showing diminished fluorescent intensities as the thickness of MPD is increased. In order to quantify the performance, the fluorescent spectra of the filtered solutions were recorded (Fig. 4(b)) and exclusion performance of each membrane was plotted by comparing the fluorescent intensity values at 515.9nm (Fig. 4(c)), where the maximum peak of initial PS latex dispersion is observed. Higher than 99.9% PS nanoparticles could be excluded from the dispersion with the membranes prepared by the areal mass higher than 2.6mg/cm2. The size exclusion performance of the BNNT membrane was also double-checked using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs, 27.1 ± 6.5nm, Fig. S7). The absorbance intensity due to the surface plasmon resonance of the AuNPs (peak maximum at 532nm), which is proportionally related to the concentration of nanoparticles in solution, was dramatically reduced after filtration of the dispersion through MPD2.1 as calculated exclusion efficiency of more than 99% (Fig. S7(c)).

Compared to other nanomaterials including CNTs, the superior thermal stability of BNNTs against both oxidation and degradation up to 900°C in air, which is much higher than the temperature for calcination of organic materials (~ 450°C), is their unique advantage in producing high-performance, regenerable membranes. To demonstrate more practical and potential application, the MPDs were regenerated via thermal calcination (450°C for 6h) of the filtered cake of PS latex as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(a). Even after the thermal calcination, no morphology change in the BNNTs on the MPD was observed (Fig. 5(b)) and the size exclusion performance of the MPDs was maintained up to 10 cycles without any significant fluctuation (Fig. 5(a)). In particular, the MPD3.1 showed the highest exclusion and the most stable performance through the tests. The excellent thermal stability of BNNT membranes is clearly in contrast to the performance of the CNT membrane fabricated via filtration of CNT dispersion (Fig. 5(c)) [59,60]. The CNT membrane in Fig. 5(d) was delaminated from the substrate and distorted significantly due to the thermal stress and combustion of CNTs through the same calcination condition (450°C for 6h). These results strongly indicate the superiority of our MPDs even under mechanical and thermal stress during the repeated cycles of filtration and calcination. As another demonstration of potential applications, nano- and micro-dusts from the environmental air were successfully collected via the vacuum-assisted filtration, and the MPD3.1 also could be regenerated by full combustion of organic particle matters (Fig. S8). We believe that our thermally regenerable MPDs with ultrafiltration performance successfully demonstrate a great potential to resolve the classical problems of ultrafiltration such as clogging of the nanopores (or fouling) and corresponding pressure drop in industrial filtration by thermal regeneration and reuse.

In this work, stable dispersions of BNNTs in organic solvents were prepared via P4VP-assisted steric stabilization of BNNTs. The excellent efficiency of P4VP in dispersing BNNTs with long term stability was clearly shown by both theoretical calculations and systematic experiments. The highly individualized BNNTs by P4VP were employed to fabricate an ultrafiltration membrane having nanopores by simple vacuum filtration of the dispersion. The membrane had high permeation flux with excellent size exclusion properties as evidenced by the investigation of using both fluorescent-labeled PS latex of about 25nm in diameter. Finally, the superior mechanical strength and the stability of the BNNT membranes against thermal oxidation were clearly demonstrated by consecutive recycling processes using repeated filtration and calcination without loss of ultrafiltration efficiency. We believe that the P4VP-assisted BNNT dispersion may inspire researchers working in a variety of fields who want to accommodate the unique function of BNNTs but had problems in dispersing the materials. In addition, the concept of thermally durable and reusable ultrafiltration membrane fabricated via a simple protocol resolves problems such as clogging (or fouling) and corresponding pressure drop, which are common in water purification and micro- or nano-particle removal by filtration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purification of BNNTs and preparation of BNNT dispersions
ce:section.ce:para: As-received BNNTs (BNNT LLC.) were calcined at 650°C for 6h in air to oxidize amorphous boron impurity (α-boron). The oxidized α-borons were washed out by a three times of bath sonication and filtration cycle with methanol. During the filtration process, additional attention was paid to avoid complete drying of the BNNTs as a form of bucky paper because it was much harder to re-disperse the completely dried BNNT mats compared to as-received BNNT cocoons. Then, the washed BNNTs were freeze dried using mixed solution of tert-butyl alcohol and DI water (w/w = 40/60) for 2 days as a form of fluffy cotton for further use. The BNNT dispersions with various solvents and concentrations were prepared in the presence/absence of P4VP under bath sonication (KODO ultrasonic cleaner, 20L, 350W) for 1h. The BNNT dispersions without purification of α-boron were also prepared with the same procedure.

The BNNT dispersions for UV–vis characterization were prepared in methanol with various P4VP to BNNT mass ratio (MP4VP / MBNNT) values ranging from 0 to 3.0 (C BNNT = 0.2mg/mL) by bath sonication for 1h. For Turbiscan characterization, the BNNT dispersions in methanol in the presence/absence of P4VP were prepared (C BNNT = 0.792mg/mL) by the same procedure for UV–vis samples. Samples for Turbiscan (20mL vial) were characterized with a scan rate of one scan perhour.

The BNNT membranes with different areal mass of BNNTs were fabricated by filtering calculated volume of P4VP-assisted BNNT dispersion (as-received BNNTs without purification of boron impurity, MP4VP / MBNNT = 0.5) on glass-fiber prefilter (Millipore APFF 04700, pore size ~ 0.7m, 4.7cm in diameter) under transmembrane pressure of 1bar (Wheaton membrane filtration kit, filtered circular diameter of 3.5cm). After complete dryness, the membrane was calcined in a furnace at 450°C for 6h for removal of P4VP. The color of the membrane was turned to brighter gray from metallic dark gray after calcination due to oxidation of some α-boron impurities. The BNNT membrane without the use of P4VP was prepared by filtering methanol BNNT dispersion sonicated for 1h through the glass-fiber prefilter.

The thickness values of BNNT membranes were measured by analysis of fractured membrane images taken by scanning electron microscopy (FEI, NOVASEM 450). For precise measurement, the BNNT membranes were embedded into epoxy resin and cut by microtome. (RMC, PT-PC PowerTome Ultramicrotomes) The hydraulic permeability was characterized by measuring the time for filtering a fixed amount of pure water (10mL) through BNNT membranes having specific filtering area (about 2cm2).

As-received fluorescent-labeled PS latex dispersion (purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, carboxylate-modified and fluorescent yellow-green labeled, average diameter ~ 25nm) was diluted with pure water (0.008wt% fluorescent-labeled PS latex) and used for filtration experiment (actual filtered circular diameter is ca. 1.6cm). For pretreatment, the membranes were rinsed with 1mL of methanol and 1mL of DI water consecutively. Then, the diluted fluorescent-labeled PS latex (7.5mL) was filtered under transmembrane pressure of 0.8bar. The filtered aqueous solution was collected for further characterization. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs, average diameter of about 27nm) were synthesized with trisodium citrate [61]. Briefly, 20mL of 1.0mM gold(III) chloride trihydrate (Sigma-Aldrich) was poured into 50mL Erlenmeyer flask. When the solution is boiled under magnetic stirring on hot-plate, 1mL solution of trisodium citrate dehydrate (1wt%, Sigma-Aldrich) was added. The color of solution was turned to deep red rapidly and the solution was cooled down to room temperature after 10min of boiling. The size of AuNPs was measured by image analysis of SEM images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of size exclusion efficiency of membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent intensity of filtered solution from PS latex dispersion was recorded by fluorescent microscopy (SCINCO, FluoroMate FS-2, excitation wavelength: 455nm, emission wavelength range from 470nm to 600nm, excitation slit: 2.5nm, emission slit: 5nm). The filtering efficiency was calculated by comparing the fluorescence intensity of initial and filtered PS latexes. For the characterization of AuNPs, absorption intensities, measured at 532nm by UV–vis spectroscopy, of initial AuNPs and filtered solution were compared.

The fluorescent-labeled PS latex (7.5mL of 0.008wt% solid content) was filtered through three BNNT membranes (MPD2.1, MPD2.6, and MPD3.1). The collected filtered solutions were characterized by fluorescent microscopy to estimate the exclusion efficiency of the membranes. Then, the cakes of PS latex were calcined at 450°C for 6h for next cycle of filtration and regeneration. The cycles were repeated 10 times for each membrane.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from Korea Institute of Science and Technology open research program (KIST ORP), Nano·Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (2016M3A7B4905619), and the Graphene Materials/Components Development Project (10044366) through the Ministry of Trade, Industry, and Energy (MOTIE), Republic of Korea.

ce:section.ce:para: B.J.K., J.K. and S.G.J. planned and designed the experiments and prepared the manuscript. M.J.K. provided purified BNNTs. H.Y. provided scientific discussion on colloidal dispersion and revised the manuscript. H.L. prepared BNNT dispersion and carried out characterization. H.L. and S.G.J. prepared BNNT membranes and characterized the filtration performance. B.L.S. performed theoretical calculation. The corresponding authors J.K., B.J.K., and S.G.J supervised this work. All authors discussed the results and implications and commented on the manuscript at all stages.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Computational methods, thermodynamic solubility parameters, UV–vis analysis, size distribution of diameter of BNNTs and PS latex, optical images of BNNT dispersions and set of filtration test, and SEM image of the cross-sectional BNNT membrane, PS latex, and gold nanoparticles. (PDF).
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.memsci.2018.01.030.
